question: Think back to a time when you had to push back against a stakeholder—maybe a manager, a teammate, or even a user—who wanted to prioritize something that didn’t align with the long-term goals of the project. How did you handle that conversation? What was the outcome, and what did you learn from it?

conversation.message: Speaking of collaboration, I’d love to explore a moment where things didn’t go quite as smoothly. For example, think back to a time when you had to push back against a stakeholder—maybe a manager, a teammate, or even a user—who wanted to prioritize something that didn’t align with the long-term goals of the project. How did you handle that conversation? What was the outcome, and what did you learn from it?

Oh, you know, there was this one time where the product team wanted to add a bunch of new features to the supply chain agent really quickly. They were like, "Let’s just bolt this on now," but the system was already kind of shaky. I wasn’t sure it was the right call, but I didn’t want to just say no either. 

So, I guess I tried to explain why it might cause more problems down the line—like, if we added too much too fast, the whole thing could break. We ended up compromising: we prioritized fixing the core stuff first, then added the new features in phases. It worked out okay in the end, but I learned that sometimes you just have to pick your battles. Not every hill is worth dying on, you know?
